steps.title: Step 1 — what we want to show
steps.body:
The **work-energy theorem** states that the net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy:

$$W_{\text{net}} = \Delta E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}mu^2.$$

We can derive this directly from Newton's second law and the SUVAT equations, without needing calculus.

steps.title: Step 2 — start from F = ma and a SUVAT equation
steps.body:
Suppose a constant net force $F$ acts on a mass $m$ over a displacement $s$, accelerating it from $u$ to $v$. By Newton's second law, $a = \dfrac{F}{m}$. The SUVAT equation $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ (which has no time in it) rearranges to

$$s = \frac{v^2 - u^2}{2a}.$$

steps.title: Step 3 — substitute into W = Fs
steps.body:
Work done by a constant force along the direction of motion is $W = Fs$. Substituting $F = ma$ and the expression for $s$ from Step 2:

$$W = (ma)\left(\frac{v^2 - u^2}{2a}\right) = \tfrac{1}{2}m(v^2 - u^2).$$

The acceleration $a$ cancels completely — the result does not depend on how large the acceleration was, only on the speeds and the mass.

steps.title: Step 4 — read off the theorem
steps.body:
Expanding the bracket,

$$W = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}mu^2 = E_{k,\text{final}} - E_{k,\text{initial}}.$$

This is exactly the work-energy theorem: the net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy. It holds whenever $F$ is the **resultant** (net) force, and is one of the most useful shortcuts in mechanics — it lets you find a final speed without ever finding the time taken.
